symptom: VM deployment times out after ~20 minutes with ProvisioningTimeout error. Boot diagnostics shows Windows OOBE wizard screen. VM not accessible via RDP/SSH.
rootCause: A generalized VHD (sysprep'd/waagent-deprovisioned) was deployed using specialized deployment method (Attach). The provisioning agent does not run, and the guest OS is stuck waiting for OOBE input (Windows) or unconfigured (Linux).
solution: Redeploy the VM using the correct method: use FromImage (generalized) instead of Attach (specialized). Check ASC → VM → CreateOption to verify. Auto RCAs via Execution Graphs can detect this mismatch.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/DEPLOYMENT/## Common Scenarios/1. Generalized_Specialized/1.2 Generalized_Specialized mismatch.md

symptom: VM deployment shows ProvisioningTimeout error, but VM is accessible via RDP/SSH and usable. VM shows failed status in Azure portal and Get-Sub.
rootCause: A specialized VHD (production OS instance) was deployed using generalized method (FromImage). The provisioning agent tries to push configuration but the OS was not prepared for it (not sysprep'd/deprovisioned).
solution: Recreate the VM with specialized deployment method (Attach instead of FromImage). Check ASC → VM → CreateOption to diagnose. Auto RCAs via Execution Graphs can identify this Gen/Spec mismatch.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/DEPLOYMENT/## Common Scenarios/1. Generalized_Specialized/1.2 Generalized_Specialized mismatch.md

symptom: VM deployment or resize fails with AllocationFailed or SkuNotAvailable error.
rootCause: Insufficient cluster capacity in the target region/zone, or the requested VM size is not available in that location.
solution: 1) Try a different VM size; 2) Try a different region or availability zone; 3) Deallocate existing VMs in the allocation group first; 4) Refer to allocation failure troubleshooting: https://docs.azure.cn/zh-cn/virtual-machines/troubleshooting/allocation-failure and resize troubleshooting: https://docs.azure.cn/zh-cn/virtual-machines/troubleshooting/restart-resize-error-troubleshooting
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/DEPLOYMENT/## Common Scenarios/2. Capacity and size limit.md

symptom: Need to investigate disk state transitions during VM operations (attach/detach/redeploy); disk may appear stuck in Reserved or Unattached state unexpectedly
rootCause: DiskRP manages disk lifecycle events (Attach, Move, UpdateDiskMetadata) during CRP VM operations. State transitions follow: Unattached -> Reserved -> Attached (or reverse). Unexpected states indicate CRP-DiskRP coordination issues.
solution: Query DiskRPResourceLifecycleEvent on disksmc.kusto.chinacloudapi.cn/Disks: filter by resourceName, project TIMESTAMP/activityId/callerName/diskEvent/state/storageAccountType. Cross-reference with DiskManagerApiQoSEvent using operationId to get subscription/httpStatusCode/requestEntity details.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/DEPLOYMENT/DiskRP Kusto.md

symptom: Questions about RHUI support scope, ICM template, and Azure China RHUI repos for RedHat images
rootCause: RHUI in Azure China uses the same repos and documentation as public Azure; RHUI PG support scope is limited to RHUI infrastructure and RedHat Azure image configuration (WALA/serial console/CI), not core OS functionality
solution: 1) Use same RHUI docs as public Azure: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/workloads/redhat/redhat-rhui; 2) Raise ICM via template: https://portal.microsofticm.com/imp/v3/incidents/create?tmpl=q1Ck2J; 3) RHUI support scope: RHUI infra + RedHat Azure image config only; core OS issues go to respective teams; 4) Image name: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.7 (LVM) - x64 Gen 2
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/New feature landing/RedHat image for Adobe- April 2023.md

symptom: Terraform VM deployment from 3rd-party marketplace image in Mooncake fails with 500 InternalServerError
rootCause: Terraform plan block triggers ARM to call storeapi.azure.com (public Azure service) which is not reachable from Mooncake ARM instances, returning 403 to ARM, which then returns 500 to customer. ARM never sends request to CRP.
solution: Remove the plan block from terraform azurerm_virtual_machine resource arguments. The plan block is used by public Azure for 3rd-party marketplace image billing verification and is not supported in Mooncake.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/2-7 Deployment failure, 500 internal server error.md

symptom: Multiple VMs on same node go down with VhdDiskPrt Event 16 errors, Hyper-V Event 18590 fatal error, VMs become unresponsive and fail to boot
rootCause: SSD data drive failure on the physical node. In public Azure, Anvil would automatically detect the failure and send the node OFR (Out for Repair). However, Mooncake policy does not support Anvil auto-repair, so the node remains in service with failed drive.
solution: Issue FC request (e.g., FC63019) to push the affected node OFR for drive replacement. Customer can reset VMs via console to trigger live migration to healthy nodes as immediate workaround.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/2.1-OverAllocationRequest[VM] VM reboot RCA E16.md

symptom: VM unexpected reboot requiring RCA; customer requests root cause analysis for VM downtime
rootCause: Host OS crash on the physical node caused all VMs on the node to reboot. The VM availability impacts report in ASC shows the detailed RCA including impact begin/end timestamps.
solution: Check ASC VM page for Detailed RCA. Provide customer-facing RCA using standard Chinese template (do NOT share detailed failure signature as it is MS confidential). VM is auto-restored after host reboot.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/2.2-2110080040000963 - VM Unexpected Reboot.md

symptom: Multiple VMs experience network jitter/connectivity loss simultaneously; two VMs become completely unavailable. VMs not in same availability set but co-located on same node.
rootCause: Host node became unhealthy - Azure Fabric was unable to reach the node, causing all VMs on that node to lose connectivity or become unavailable.
solution: Use Kusto LogContainerSnapshot to find containerId+nodeId by subscriptionId+roleInstanceName, then check node health state. If node is unhealthy and fabric cannot reach it, the issue is platform-side (host node failure). VMs recover after node recovery or live migration. Confirm via ASC health state and node events.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/2.3-2110120040000615 - 多台虚拟机发生网络抖动，两台虚拟机已经宕机.md

symptom: Linux VMs fail SSH connection intermittently; VMs become unresponsive and require portal restart to recover. OOM killer triggered in system logs, no logs between OOM event and reboot (VM hung).
rootCause: VM disk IO throttling caused system hang. OS disk was Standard HDD (max 60MB/s), data disks were Standard SSD (max 60MB/s). During issue time, all disks read/write bandwidth exceeded limits, causing IO starvation and OOM.
solution: Check ASC for disk SKU and IO metrics. If disk read/write bandwidth consistently exceeds SKU limits, upgrade disk tier (Standard HDD/SSD → Premium SSD) or resize VM to support higher IO. Check Geneva VM dashboard for CPU/memory/disk metrics correlation. Review system logs for OOM killer events as indicator of resource exhaustion.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/2.4-2201110040000700 VM failed SSH connection.md

symptom: Turbo boost CPU frequency information not displayed in Msv2/Mdsv2 series VMs. Commands turbostat and cpupower show no frequency data, while Dv5/Ev5 VMs show it correctly.
rootCause: By design. Msv2/Mdsv2 VMs are hosted on older Gen servers where Hyper-V does not expose turbo information to guest VM. Turbo IS enabled on host but not visible inside VM. Dv5/Ev5 series on newer hardware DO expose this info.
solution: Inform customer this is expected behavior (by design). Turbo boost is enabled at host level but not visible via guest OS commands on older VM series. Reference: osgwiki.com/wiki/BestGuestRCA/CPU_freq_powermgt. If customer needs turbo visibility, consider migrating to Dv5/Ev5 series VMs. Use isolated VM SKUs for single-core turbo workloads.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/2.6-2211190040000183 - The turbo boost CPU frequen.md

symptom: VMSS/AKS node pool creation fails with allocation failure error despite sufficient SKU capacity shown in AllocableVmCount Kusto query
rootCause: The target compute cluster is not onboarded to AzSM (Azure Smart Allocation). When Fabric.AllocationServiceMode != 6 the cluster cannot serve AKS node pool creation requests even with available capacity.
solution: 1) Verify allocation failure via VMApiQosEvent Kusto. 2) Get activityId and check ComputeAllocationActivity for failing tenantName. 3) Confirm not AzSM by querying TMMgmtFabricSettingEtwTable for Fabric.AllocationServiceMode (value != 6). 4) Raise ICM to PG to onboard cluster to AzSM. Ref ICM: 483696265. Note: NewDeployment = new; Upgrade = restart/deallocate-start.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/VMSS - failed to create AKS node pool.md

symptom: Ubuntu 24.04 VM needs account lockout configuration: lock login for N minutes after N failed attempts
rootCause: Ubuntu 24.04 defaults to pam_faillock (replacing older pam_tally2). Three PAM config files need modification: /etc/security/faillock.conf, /etc/pam.d/common-auth, /etc/pam.d/common-account.
solution: 1) Edit /etc/security/faillock.conf: deny=3, unlock_time=600, fail_interval=900, even_deny_root, root_unlock_time=900. 2) Edit /etc/pam.d/common-auth: add auth required pam_faillock.so preauth before pam_unix, add auth [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail after pam_unix. 3) Edit /etc/pam.d/common-account: add account required pam_faillock.so.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/[2508270010002646]Ubuntu 24.04 unlock configuratio.md

symptom: Azure Dedicated Host VMs experience frequent unexpected reboots (8 in 3 months), all caused by physical node disk errors on same cluster
rootCause: Physical node disks (SKhynix HFS960G32MED-3410A) reached End-of-Life with SMART threshold exceeded (FaultCode 32010 DISK_ER_CDF10). CSI diagnostics detect disk failures triggering node fault, forcing VM service healing to healthy node.
solution: 1) Check LogContainerSnapshot for nodeId changes. 2) Check LogNodeSnapshot for nodeState=HumanInvestigate. 3) Check AzureDCMDb.ResourceSnapshotHistoryV1 for FaultCode 32010 and disk details. 4) Raise ICM to EEE for disk replacement timeline. Ref ICM: 405079845.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/3. Azure Dedicated Host/3.1 - 2307050030002556 - Physic Node disk EOL.md

symptom: AKS nodes report VMEventScheduled condition with Freeze event via IMDS; event persists 15+ minutes though actual freeze is only ~9 seconds
rootCause: IMDS scheduled event appears when platform initiates host maintenance (Network impact: Freeze). AKS node-problem-detector polls IMDS every minute. Event transitions Scheduled->Started->completed but remains visible until maintenance fully completes.
solution: 1) Verify via AzPE Kusto: query AzPEWorkflowSnapshot/AzPEWorkflowEvent with nodeId and vmName to trace full workflow lifecycle. 2) Impact is minimal (~9 seconds freeze). 3) Event clears when maintenance completes. CME accounts have AzPE Kusto permission.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/2. Azure VM/VM - scheduled events.md

symptom: Mooncake RHEL VM: Only selected data disk for Azure Disk Encryption, but after encryption both OS and data disk show as encrypted
rootCause: Mooncake RHEL images (provided by 21Vianet) use LVM for OS disk (/dev/mapper/rhel-root mounted at /root). The ADE extension checks if mount source contains /dev/mapper AND mount target is /root to determine if OS disk is encrypted, causing a false positive on these images.
solution: This is a known limitation of Mooncake RHEL images. The OS disk is not actually encrypted - it is a false positive due to LVM configuration. CentOS or Global Azure RHEL images (first-party) do not have this issue as their OS disk is not LVM-based. No action needed if only data disk encryption was intended.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/4. Linux on Azure/4.1-118121719461691 - 云角redhat磁盘加密问题 -Jessie.md

symptom: Linux VM cannot boot: Kernel panic - VFS: Unable to mount root fs on unknown-block(0,0). Serial log shows BOOT_IMAGE=/vmlinuz-3.10.0-1160.15.2.el7.x86_64
rootCause: Missing initramfs file for the current boot kernel version. The initramfs (initial RAM disk) required by the kernel to mount root filesystem is absent or corrupted.
solution: 1) Create snapshot of OS disk, attach to rescue Linux VM; 2) Use chroot to access the filesystem (follow MS docs for RHEL/CentOS LVM chroot); 3) Check /boot dir to confirm which initramfs files exist; 4) If another kernel version has valid initramfs, switch GRUB default: run awk to list menu entries, edit /etc/default/grub to set GRUB_DEFAULT=<working-kernel-index>; 5) Regenerate grub config: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg; 6) Unmount, swap snapshot disk back to VM. Alternative: Ask 21Vianet to open console and select working kernel from grub menu.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/4. Linux on Azure/4.10-Change boot sequence by using chroot for fail.md

symptom: SAP NFS server VMs (GlusterFS cluster on SUSE 12) experienced unexpected reboots/redeployments, causing NFS service disruption for SAP HANA workload
rootCause: Platform-initiated VM reboot/redeploy during maintenance. Multiple NFS server nodes were rebooted at different times (node1 at 6:15 UTC, node2 at 6:58 UTC), while node3 had no reboot. The reboots caused tenant migration between physical nodes.
solution: Troubleshooting approach: 1) Check VM Dashboard in Geneva for nodeId/containerId changes (tenant migration = platform reboot); 2) Check ASI for disk IO errors on physical nodes; 3) Check XPortal latency report - P99 IO latency below 54ms is normal; 4) Verify network connectivity between NFS server and client VMs. For prevention: consider Availability Zones or PPG for SAP NFS cluster.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/4. Linux on Azure/2305010040000498  VM SAP NFS.md

symptom: Ubuntu Linux VM cannot be accessed: SSH fails, VMAccess agent does not work, VM cannot obtain IP; serial console password reset unavailable on Ubuntu
rootCause: Ubuntu does not support password reset via serial console natively; when both VMAccess extension and SSH are broken and VM has no IP connectivity, standard password reset methods fail
solution: Use az vm repair to create a recovery VM with OS disk attached as data disk. Mount the root filesystem at /recovery, blank the password field in /etc/shadow using sed, unmount, swap OS disk back. Then login via serial console (press Enter at password prompt), set new password with passwd command. Key steps: (1) az vm repair create, (2) mount /dev/sdc1 /recovery, (3) sed to blank password in /recovery/etc/shadow, (4) az vm repair restore, (5) serial console login + passwd
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/4. Linux on Azure/4.9-Reset password by using a recovery VM.md

symptom: Linux VM provisioning times out when created via SDK/ARM template with Chinese characters in resource tags; portal deployment without Chinese tags works fine. cloud-init.log shows FAIL on _get_metadata_from_imds with error: module object has no attribute JSONDecodeError
rootCause: IMDS returns JSON response containing Chinese characters from tags. On CentOS 7.x with Python 2.7, cloud-init catches json.decoder.JSONDecodeError which does not exist in Python 2.7 (only available in Python 3.5+), causing an AttributeError that crashes cloud-init metadata crawl. This prevents provisioning from completing, leading to waagent waiting indefinitely for ovf-env.xml
solution: Remove Chinese characters from VM tags (use ASCII-only tag values). Alternatively, upgrade to a newer image with Python 3 and updated cloud-init. Note: VMs created from improperly deprovisioned images (with leftover ovf-env.xml) may accidentally work around this bug since waagent finds the old ovf-env.xml immediately. Proper fix requires Python 3 + cloud-init >= 20.x
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/4. Linux on Azure/q4.8-2111220040000794 Provision timeout when creat.md

symptom: Windows VM login fails with error 'The user profile service service failed the logon. User profile cannot be loaded'
rootCause: User profile corruption or permission issues on profile-related files/folders, e.g. SQM files or Exchange Server folder permissions under C:\Users\Default\AppData\Local\Microsoft\
solution: 1) Follow KB947215 troubleshooting steps; 2) Check Event Log for related errors; 3) Delete problematic SQM files or fix permissions on C:\Users\Default\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Exchange Server\v15\; 4) Use Process Monitor with runas command to simulate login and filter for Access Denied entries
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.2-"the user profile service service failed, the.md

symptom: RDP connection to Citrix VM fails with Remote Desktop license server error and license store creation failed with access denied
rootCause: Citrix XenApp uses ICA protocol which consumes RD licenses even for admin connections. MSLicensing registry key lacks Everyone Full Control permission
solution: 1) Verify licensing mode is correct (set to 4=per user); 2) Ensure SpecifiedLicenseServers points to correct license server IP; 3) Reboot; 4) Grant Everyone Full Control on HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\MSLicensing via PowerShell; 5) Reboot
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.3-RDP citrix VM 遍到License server問題.md

symptom: Questions about Windows 10 client image support lifecycle and availability on Azure Mooncake/Fairfax sovereign clouds
rootCause: Windows client images are not published to Mooncake or Fairfax because those systems cannot verify Visual Studio Subscriptions. EOL Windows versions no longer supported
solution: 1) Advise upgrade to latest supported Windows version; 2) Win10 client images only on Global Azure with MSDN subscription; 3) Not available on Mooncake/Fairfax
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.4-Windows 10 image support policy.md

symptom: Azure VM is hung/unresponsive, cannot RDP, need to generate memory dump for troubleshooting
rootCause: VM in hung state where normal remote access is unavailable
solution: Use Azure Portal Serial Console NMI: 1) Pre-configure: reg add CrashControl /v CrashDumpEnabled=1, DumpFile=C:\Windows\Memory.dmp, NMICrashDump=1; set pagefile size=RAM+200MB; 2) Ensure C: has enough space; 3) Trigger NMI from Portal; 4) OS dumps memory and restarts; 5) Upload dump for analysis
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.5-Generate dump from Azure Portal Serial console.md

symptom: Need to track which process or user is modifying specific Windows registry keys on Azure VM
rootCause: Registry changes by unknown processes causing configuration drift; standard logs do not capture registry mods by default
solution: 1) gpedit.msc -> Advanced Audit Policy -> Object Access -> Audit Registry -> enable; 2) regedit -> right-click key -> Permissions -> Advanced -> Auditing -> Add Everyone; 3) Event Viewer -> Security -> filter Task Category=Registry; Note: CurrentControlSet audits show as ControlSet001
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.6 How to enable audit for registry keys.md

symptom: Need to perform Windows Server in-place upgrade on Azure VM (e.g. 2012R2->2016/2019, 2016->2019/2022, 2019->2022)
rootCause: Windows Server in-place upgrade requires following specific supported upgrade paths (max 2 version jumps); Core->Core, Desktop->Desktop only
solution: 1) Always snapshot before upgrade; 2) Supported: 2012->2012R2/2016, 2012R2->2016/2019, 2016->2019/2022, 2019->2022; 3) Core->Core, Desktop Experience->Desktop Experience; 4) Cluster OS Rolling Upgrade: 1 version only; 5) Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows-in-place-upgrade
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.7 Windows in-place upgrade.md

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD caused by CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor update (csagent.sys) on 2024-07-19, VM boot loops
rootCause: CrowdStrike channel file C-00000291*.sys with timestamp 2024-07-19 0409 UTC was problematic; hosts online before 0527 UTC affected; Mac/Linux not affected
solution: Auto: BSOD-Autofix.ps1 with az vm repair (handles encrypted/unmanaged). Manual: 1) Snapshot; 2) Create managed disk; 3) Attach to repair VM; 4) Delete E:\Windows\System32\drivers\CrowdStrike\C-00000291*.sys; 5) Detach; 6) Swap OS disk. Impact: timestamp 0409=bad, 0527+=good
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.8 How to fix BSOD issue on Azure VM.md

symptom: After CrowdStrike BSOD fix, Azure VM fails to boot with winload.exe error 0xC000000E or winload.efi error 0xc0000225
rootCause: BCD corruption or incorrect OS disk reference after OS disk swap during CrowdStrike remediation
solution: 0xC000000E: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/azure/virtual-machines/windows/error-code-0xc000000e; 0xc0000225: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/azure/virtual-machines/windows/boot-error-status-not-found; Black screen with no error: mount disk in Hyper-V, specify boot file, verify VM boots, then swap OS disk back
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.8 How to fix BSOD issue on Azure VM.md

symptom: During CrowdStrike BSOD repair, BitLocker-encrypted disk requires recovery key that customer cannot find
rootCause: BitLocker recovery key location depends on join type: AD-joined, AAD-joined, or Azure VM
solution: AD: Get-ADObject -Filter msFVE-RecoveryInformation on DC/RSAT server. AAD (Mooncake): Connect-MgGraph -Environment china; Get-MgInformationProtectionBitlockerRecoveryKey. Routing: On-prem AD->Windows SHA; On-prem AAD->Azure AD team; Azure VM->Azure VM team
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.8 How to fix BSOD issue on Azure VM.md

symptom: When performing Swap OS disk on Azure VM, newly created Managed Disk is not visible in selection list
rootCause: Mismatch in Availability Zone configuration between original OS disk and new Managed Disk
solution: Match the Availability Zone setting of original OS disk exactly when creating repair disk. If original had no AZ, do not select any AZ for new disk
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/5. Windows on Azure/5.8 How to fix BSOD issue on Azure VM.md

symptom: VMSS has failed instances that cannot be deleted or modified via portal/CLI. az vmss delete-instances returns InternalExecutionError. VMSS state shows abnormal with multiple Failed instances.
rootCause: Specific VM instances stuck in inconsistent state: some return InstanceIdsListInstanceIdNotActiveVM (not active), others return OperationNotAllowedOnVMScaleSetVMInstanceStillProvisioning (still provisioning). These bad instances block all VMSS operations including delete.
solution: 1) Query CRP ApiQosEvent to identify which instances return BadRequest/errors. 2) Check LogContainerHealthSnapshot to confirm which instances are actually UP. 3) Raise ICM to PG to confirm action plan for stuck instances. 4) PG confirms problematic instances, customer deletes them and VMSS returns to normal. Ref ICM: 269179884.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/6. Virtual Machine Scale sets(VMSS)/6.2-Bad instances cause VMSS state abnormal.md

symptom: Mooncake 应用完成 AAD 登录后，持续重复调用 login.microsoftonline.com/common/discovery/instance 和 openid-configuration 接口，导致应用性能下降
rootCause: 应用使用 MSAL 库获取 token 时，代码实现导致登录完成后仍反复触发 metadata discovery 请求
solution: 检查 MSAL token 获取代码，确保使用 token cache 避免重复 discovery 调用；参考 MSAL 官方文档优化 token acquisition 逻辑
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/7. Fabric/2403120040001075 AAD MCD performance.md

symptom: Palo Alto firewall VM fails to convert from unmanaged disk to managed disk; extensions (OMSAgentForLinux, LinuxDiagnostics, backup) stuck in transitioning/failed state; az vm extension remove/delete times out
rootCause: Azure VM extensions are not in the support matrix for Palo Alto OS. Extension operations (install/remove) are expected to fail or hang on non-standard OS like Palo Alto firewall appliances.
solution: 1) Attempt extension removal: az vm extension delete -g <RG> --vm-name <VM> -n <ExtensionName> — success not guaranteed on Palo Alto OS. 2) Follow Palo Alto official KB (kA14u0000008UlWCAU): recommended approach is to rebuild the VM. 3) Unmanaged disk retirement deadline extended to 2026-03-31 (Azure China). 4) waagent may report NotReady — expected on non-endorsed Linux distros.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/Palo Alto VM unable to convert to managed disk.md

symptom: Customer accidentally deleted Azure storage account, blob container, or unmanaged VM disk and needs recovery
rootCause: Accidental deletion; recovery depends on replication type and whether resource was recreated with same name. GC runs within 0-15 days after deletion
solution: Storage accounts (ARM/ASM): escalate to CSS. Blob containers (GRS/RA-GRS only, not recreated with same name): escalate to WASU via ICM Sev.3/Sev.2. Unsupported: LRS containers, queues, individual blobs, managed disks/snapshots/images, partial data. Warn customer NOT to recreate with same name before recovery attempt. For Mooncake container recovery, recovered data lands in secondary region.
sourceRef: MCVKB/wiki_migration/======VM&SCIM======/##deprecated 6.19 Overall_ How to recover deleted.md

symptom: Customer deleted managed disk and needs recovery; disk was in soft-delete state
rootCause: Managed disk soft-delete feature retains deleted disk blobs for up to 4 days. Conditions: disk created >10 days ago, deleted <4 days (96 hours) ago, not Ultra Disk. Internal only: soft-delete auto-enabled after 10 days in production.
solution: 1) Check SoftDelete event via Kusto (DiskRPResourceLifecycleEvent table on disksmc cluster) or Azure Support Center. 2) If SoftDelete confirmed: submit ICM to AzureRT/Disk Service — Sev.2 template: u162Ue, Sev.3 template: i1yp18. Set incident type to 'Customer Reported'. 3) If no SoftDelete event: collect blob URL and pseudosubscription, forward ICM to XStore/Table Server. Note: since 2022-04-01, old Jarvis/Escort process is deprecated; use direct ICM to PG.
sourceRef: MCVKB/wiki_migration/======VM&SCIM======/##deprecated##6.18 _ MCVKB _ How to recover soft d.md

symptom: Customer deleted managed disk and needs recovery; disk was hard deleted (not in soft-delete state)
rootCause: Managed disk was hard deleted — either soft-delete conditions not met (disk created <10 days, deleted >4 days ago, or Ultra Disk) or SoftDelete event not present in logs
solution: Open Sev.2 or Sev.3 ICM to XStore/Table Server team. ICM template: O01O2z. If no direct access to route ICM, ask TA to route or email cssstgrec@microsoft.com. Recovery not guaranteed and depends on storage backend GC status.
sourceRef: MCVKB/wiki_migration/======VM&SCIM======/##deprecated##6.18 _ MCVKB _ How to recover soft d.md

symptom: NVv3 VM (Nvidia Tesla M60) GPU issues with GRID driver version 17.x — driver incompatibility
rootCause: GRID Driver version 17.x is incompatible with NVv3 series VMs using Tesla M60 GPU
solution: Follow TSG: AzureIaaSVM wiki 'NVv3 (Nvidia Tesla M60) GRID Driver version 17.x Incompatible_AGEX'. Downgrade or use compatible GRID driver version.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/Training & Brownbags/14 GPU Extension Sharing.md

symptom: Unable to use GPU after enabling Remote Desktop Session Host (RDSH) role on N-series VM
rootCause: Enabling RDSH role can interfere with GPU passthrough or driver configuration on N-series VMs
solution: Follow TSG: AzureIaaSVM wiki 'Unable to use GPU after enabling the Remote Desktop Session Hosts Roles_AGEX'

symptom: NVs_v3 GPU driver disabled with error Code 43 after VM reboot
rootCause: GPU driver becomes disabled with Windows Device Manager Code 43 after reboot on NVs_v3 series VMs
solution: Follow TSG: AzureIaaSVM wiki 'NVs_v3-GPU-Driver-is-disabled-with-code-43-after-reboot_AGEX'
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/Training & Brownbags/14 GPU Extension Sharing.md

symptom: VMSS management operations (update/scale) on MR-enabled tenant hosting Service Fabric cluster stuck for several hours
rootCause: Three possible causes on MR-enabled SF cluster: (a) incorrect Service Fabric configuration settings, (b) job stuck at Policy Engine side, (c) by design — SF Gold/Silver durability behavior blocks operations during repair. Components: ARM → CRP → Fabric → IR MicroService of SF.
solution: Investigate by checking: 1) SF cluster durability tier (Gold/Silver = MR enabled); 2) Policy Engine job status; 3) SF configuration correctness. Ref ICM: 120395700.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/Training & Brownbags/2. Azure Compute EEE Brownbag.md

symptom: VMSS nodes enter failed state after Scale-in operation due to Fabric Failover
rootCause: Fabric Failover triggered during or after VMSS scale-in operation causes remaining nodes to enter failed state. Components: ARM → CRP (VMSS) → Fabricator (Fabric).
solution: Investigate Fabric Failover root cause via Kusto/ICM. Ref ICM: 120205931.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/Training & Brownbags/2. Azure Compute EEE Brownbag.md

symptom: Msv2/Mdsv2 series VM quota approved but portal still cannot create the VM (e.g. Standard_M208s_v2). Deployment fails despite quota being granted.
rootCause: PG backend configuration was not updated after quota approval. The quota was granted but the underlying platform settings for M-series in that region were not properly configured.
solution: Engage PG via ICM to perform backend configuration update. After PG updates the settings, the VM SKU becomes deployable in portal.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/[Team]Process and IcM and Common Tools/Project-based Support/1.1 2022 OPPO AWS Compete_EDA_VM deployment/Case Review.md

symptom: AWS image imported to Azure: Gen1 VM created from the image works fine, but Gen2 VM created from the same image is inaccessible (cannot connect).
rootCause: The image captured from AWS was a Gen1 image. Using it directly for Gen2 VM deployment results in boot or access failures due to UEFI/BIOS incompatibility.
solution: Capture a proper Gen2 image from AWS (ensure UEFI boot support) before importing to Azure. Do not reuse a Gen1 image for Gen2 VM creation.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/[Team]Process and IcM and Common Tools/Project-based Support/1.1 2022 OPPO AWS Compete_EDA_VM deployment/Case Review.md

symptom: Customer receives Azure planned maintenance notification with a tracking ID (SN-xxx) and wants to know which VMs/subscriptions are impacted, the reason, and timeline
solution: Search tracking ID in Iridias (https://iridias.microsoft.com/maintenance) to get notification details and associated ICM. The ICM contains: (1) internal maintenance ID and description (e.g. TOR reboot), (2) complete impact list of subscriptions and VMs as attachment, (3) the requestor who triggered the notification. Search SN-xxx ID for additional related ICMs for more context.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/How to get the impact node list of the Maintenance.md

symptom: Customer VM appears in planned maintenance impact list but may have already been live-migrated to a different node before maintenance execution
rootCause: Delay between generating the maintenance impact list and sending the notification; VM may be live-migrated to a non-impacted node during this window
solution: Verify VM current node placement using Kusto: cluster(azurecm.chinanorth2.kusto.chinacloudapi.cn).database(azurecm).LogContainerSnapshot filtered by subscriptionId and roleInstanceName, check nodeId changes over time. Compare current nodeId against nodes listed in maintenance ICM. Also verify impacted nodes status via LogNodeSnapshot - nodeAvailabilityState should be Unallocatable for nodes pending maintenance.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/How to get the impact node list of the Maintenance.md

symptom: Planned maintenance impact list from PG may miss customer VMs that are actually on the affected nodes
rootCause: PG may use incorrect Kusto query parameters when generating the subscription and VM impact list, resulting in incomplete coverage
solution: Cross-verify by querying LogContainerSnapshot filtered by nodeId (not by subscription) to enumerate ALL VMs on the affected nodes: cluster(azurecm.chinanorth2.kusto.chinacloudapi.cn).database(azurecm).LogContainerSnapshot | where nodeId in (affected_nodes). Also monitor that impacted nodes remain Unallocatable before maintenance window; if nodes become Available again, notify PG to re-mark them as Unallocatable.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/VM/SME Topics/How to get the impact node list of the Maintenance.md
